title: Why NZ Businesses Command Exit Multiples: Labour Costs as Competitive Moat
excerpt: Labour arbitrage has ended. NZ SMEs now compete on operational excellence. Why this shifts the exit multiple narrative and what acquirers actually value.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: September 2026 is prime exit season — Q4 is when the year's M&A activity closes, and businesses positioning for year-end transactions are making final decisions now. The labour cost arbitrage that sustained NZ SME pricing for two decades is rapidly closing. Wage convergence and rising AU competition means NZ businesses can no longer trade on labour savings alone. What replaces it? Operational maturity, systems, and defensible customer moats. This article explores what's actually shifted in how acquirers value NZ businesses — and why September is the right time to audit your exit readiness.

sections.heading: The labour cost arbitrage is ending
sections.body: Ten years ago, a NZ SME could undercut AU competitors on cost alone. A manufacturing or service business with $2M revenue could compete on price, and the labour savings covered thin margins. Wage inflation in NZ, combined with AU businesses investing in automation and systems, has closed that gap. An equivalent Australian business is no longer 20–30% more expensive to operate. The delta has shrunk to 5–10%, and it's still narrowing. This matters because many NZ business owners have built pricing models and customer relationships on cost leadership. When that moat crumbles, the business is suddenly exposed.

sections.heading: What acquirers now actually value in NZ SMEs
sections.body: The exit multiples of the best NZ businesses haven't collapsed. They've actually improved, but for different reasons. Acquirers now pay for: (1) Systems and repeatability. Can the business run without the owner? Is the playbook documented and transferable? (2) Customer longevity. What's the retention rate? Are relationships transactional or structural? (3) Defensibility. Does the business have a genuine moat — proprietary processes, technical know-how, exclusive partnerships — or is it just operational excellence? (4) Team depth. Is there a management layer below the owner? Can the acquirer integrate this business into a larger platform without losing key people? Labour cost advantage was simple: someone could buy the business, take the margin difference, and pocket it for 3–5 years. Modern acquirers don't work that way. They're looking for sustainable, scalable platforms. NZ businesses that have built those command 6–8x multiples, AU equivalent or better.

sections.heading: How this changes exit strategy
sections.body: If your business strategy has been 'stay lean, undercut on price, grow revenue,' you need to shift before you exit. Build systems. Document processes. Create a management layer that isn't you. Invest in customer retention, not just acquisition. These moves are expensive and time-consuming, but they're what modern acquirers actually pay for. A $3M revenue business with a COO, systems in place, and 85%+ customer retention will exit at 7–8x EBITDA. The same revenue business that's still owner-dependent, with thin margins and transactional customers, might only pull 3–4x, or worse, not attract serious acquirers at all. The good news: systemisation creates immediate operational benefit. You don't just exit better — you run a better business in the meantime.

sections.heading: The regional play: why NZ businesses can still command a premium
sections.body: Despite wage convergence, NZ businesses can still be more valuable to the right acquirer. Reasons: (1) Market access. A NZ business with established supplier relationships and customer networks in the Pacific region has something an AU business doesn't. (2) Regulatory familiarity. Commerce Act compliance, FMA registration, health & safety requirements — these are different in NZ, and an AU acquirer needs someone who knows the playbook. (3) Founder reputation. If you're a known operator in NZ with a track record, your business carries brand value that doesn't translate to spreadsheets but absolutely influences valuation. (4) Talent stability. An AU acquirer integrating a NZ business keeps the management team in place — that's worth a 0.5–1x EBITDA premium because integration risk drops. Play these cards explicitly in exit planning. Make your regulatory expertise, market relationships, and team stickiness visible to potential acquirers.

sections.heading: Preparing for 2026 exits
sections.body: If you're planning an exit in the next 2–3 years, here's what matters: (1) Get a business valuer to benchmark your EBITDA and multiples against recent comparable exits (not asking prices, actual exits). (2) Commission a systems audit: where is the business dependent on you? Can those dependencies be removed? (3) Stress-test customer retention. Run a churn analysis for the last 3 years. If retention is below 80%, make it a priority before exit — this is the #1 red flag acquirers see. (4) Build management depth. Hire or promote a CFO or COO in the 18 months before exit. This move alone can add 1–1.5x EBITDA to the purchase price. (5) Get serious about acquirer targeting. Who's actually likely to buy your business, and why? Build that story explicitly — it drives the conversation and the multiple.

title: Active Investment Planning 2026: What Changed & Why It Matters
excerpt: Recent INZ policy shifts now require different structuring for AIP visa applications. What's changed, which investors are affected, and how to adapt your strategy.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: July 2026 marks the halfway point of the year — and for Active Investor Plus visa holders, the mid-year review window is now open. INZ's 2026 policy clarifications have had six months to take effect, and the compliance picture is sharper. Some investors have already restructured; others are running out of time. The Active Investor Plus (AIP) visa pathway has been a workhorse for migrant entrepreneurs and overseas investors seeking NZ residency, but the rules have tightened meaningfully. This article covers what's changed, who remains affected, and how to respond before the second-half audit cycle begins.

sections.heading: The AIP pathway: context
sections.body: AIP is a NZ residency visa requiring: (1) at least NZD 3 million invested in qualifying NZ business investments, (2) at least NZD 1 million in a designated investment bond (treasury), and (3) active involvement in at least one investment for the duration of the visa (a key requirement). 'Active involvement' has historically meant board participation, strategic decision-making, and documented governance. Many investors interpreted this loosely: they'd be a shareholder and attend the occasional board meeting. INZ has now clarified — and in some cases, tightened — what 'active' actually means.

sections.heading: What changed in 2026
sections.body: Three substantive changes: (1) Frequency of involvement is now explicitly documented. Monthly board meetings or equivalent governance touchpoints are expected, not quarterly check-ins. Investors who show up 1–2 times a year are no longer considered 'actively involved.' (2) Decision-making authority matters. Being a passive shareholder who ratifies management decisions is different from actively contributing to strategy. INZ now wants to see evidence of the investor's input in business decisions — not management veto, but genuine participation. (3) Documentation is stricter. Minutes must show the investor's attendance, comments, and decisions. Email approvals count, but only if they're substantive and show engagement, not just rubber-stamping. Investors who don't maintain this paper trail are at audit risk when visas come up for renewal.

sections.heading: Who's affected
sections.body: Investors with existing AIP visas who are passive shareholders in their investments: at renewal or during audits, INZ may ask for evidence of active involvement. If records are thin (no attendance logs, no decision history), this could trigger a compliance conversation. Investors with portfolio companies that are entirely operator-run, with the investor taking a hands-off approach: you may need to restructure or find different governance models. Investors who were planning a light-touch approach to NZ investment: you'll need more time and involvement than you thought.

sections.heading: How to restructure now
sections.body: If you're already invested and subject to AIP, here are three paths: (1) Increase active involvement. Install yourself on the board, attend monthly meetings, document decisions. This is the simplest path if you have the bandwidth. (2) Restructure to a governance role outside the board. Some investors become strategic advisers with documented monthly touchpoints, steering calls, and approval rights on key decisions. This counts as 'active' and provides governance without full board liability. (3) Rebalance your portfolio. If one investment is truly passive, you can make another investment the focus of your 'active involvement' — but only if that second investment genuinely gets your attention. INZ doesn't accept portfolio sleight-of-hand; they want to see real engagement somewhere.

sections.heading: New investor planning
sections.body: If you're considering AIP for the first time: (1) Structure the investment from day one to be active. Choose one business as your 'active' focus. Take a board seat or equivalent governance role. (2) Plan the time commitment: realistically, expect 4–6 hours per month per active investment. (3) Document everything. Board minutes, decisions, emails, strategy notes. Build this into the governance system immediately. (4) Choose your investments carefully. You need businesses where you can add value and be genuinely engaged. A passive property investment won't cut it; you need a tradable SME where you can participate in decision-making.

sections.heading: What about passive investment visas?
sections.body: If AIP's active requirement is a burden, consider the Investor Visa (passive pathway): NZD 10 million invested for 3 years, then you can exit. No active involvement required. The catch: much higher capital, no opportunity to build a business, and a 3-year lock-in. For some investors, it's the right trade-off. For others, AIP remains the better pathway if you're comfortable with governance responsibilities.

sections.heading: Action items for 2026
sections.body: If you have an AIP visa: (1) Review your governance documentation from the last 12 months. Do you have board minutes showing your attendance and decisions? If not, start building the record now. (2) If you've been passive, increase involvement in one investment and document it going forward. (3) Consult your immigration adviser about your specific position; don't rely on this article for compliance guidance. If you're planning AIP: (1) Build active involvement into the structure before you invest. (2) Choose one business as your active focus; be realistic about your bandwidth. (3) Set up governance systems (board meetings, decision logs, strategy planning) immediately. (4) Work with your immigration adviser to ensure you're compliant from day one — it's much cheaper to get structure right upfront than to fix compliance issues later.

title: Q1 2026 M&A Outlook: Australasian Brokerage Trends & Deal Flow
excerpt: Current sentiment, vendor multiples, deal categories, and what's actually moving. Where capital is going and what businesses are getting offers.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: January 2026 opens with the Australasian M&A market telling a nuanced story. As the new year begins and Q1 deal flow resumes after the December holiday pause, we're seeing selective recovery rather than a broad-based rally. Deal activity has bounced back from the 2024–2025 downturn, but capital is flowing into specific sectors and businesses with particular characteristics — not broadly across the market. This article surveys the brokerage landscape heading into Q1, recent exit multiples, and where the gaps are for sellers positioning their businesses in 2026.

sections.heading: Headline sentiment: cautious optimism
sections.body: Major private equity firms are looking for entry opportunities again, but they're selective. Trade buyers are more active than strategic acquirers. Vendors are more realistic about multiples — the 7–8x euphoria of 2021–2022 is gone, but multiples have stabilised above 5x for quality businesses. The market is clearing: good businesses are getting offers; mediocre ones are staying in the market, and some sellers are pulling away to improve operations before trying again.

sections.heading: Sector breakdown: where capital is flowing
sections.body: Healthcare services (medical clinics, allied health, diagnostic networks): Strong demand. Aging demographics, chronic disease growth, and consolidation trends are attracting both PE and strategic buyers. Multiples: 5.5–7x EBITDA for well-run networks. Supply chain and logistics: Selective. Fleet operators, warehousing, and last-mile delivery are attracting PE, but only if the business has systems and recurring contracts. One-off courier or freight broking businesses are struggling. Multiples: 4.5–6x. Professional services (accounting, legal, consulting): Mixed. Niche practices with recurring revenue (tax, compliance) are moving. Generalist firms or those dependent on a founder-rainmaker are stalled. Multiples: 4–5.5x. SaaS and software: Still overheated for early-stage; mature SaaS with $1M+ ARR and 70%+ gross margins is attracting PE at 5–7x. Hospitality and leisure: Still distressed from pandemic. Multiples remain at 3–4x; capital is sparse unless there's a clear turnaround thesis. Franchises and retail: Weak. Consumer spend is cautious, and franchise valuations reflect that.

sections.heading: Vendor multiples: what's realistic in Q1 2026
sections.body: Premium tier (7–8x): Recurring revenue, 70%+ gross margin, documented systems, experienced management team, clear growth levers. Examples: niche SaaS, dental/medical networks, specialist logistics. Upper-mid tier (5.5–6.5x): Growing revenue, good margins, some management depth, defensible position. Most 'good businesses' fit here. Mid tier (4.5–5.5x): Stable revenue, flat growth, owner-dependent, decent margins. Requires a narrative around improvement. Lower tier (3–4.5x): Distressed, declining, or highly cyclical. Only strategic buyers (cost-saving, synergy thesis) are interested. For NZ businesses specifically: add 0.5–1x to AU comps if the business has a good local team, market access, and low integration risk. Subtract 0.5x if there's single-currency risk or if the buyer is AU-based and unfamiliar with NZ.

sections.heading: What's moving, what's stuck
sections.body: Moving fast (under 6 months): Healthcare networks, niche SaaS, specialist logistics, businesses with 20%+ YoY growth and clear buyer universe. Typically attracting 2–4 competing bidders. Moving normally (6–12 months): Upper-mid-tier stable businesses, professional services with recurring revenue, niche distribution. Usually 1–2 qualified buyers. Stuck (stalled >12 months): Founder-dependent services, businesses with declining revenue, highly competitive sectors with thin margins, anything with regulatory uncertainty or key-customer risk.

sections.heading: Deal structure trends
sections.body: Earn-outs are back. Many deals are structured as base price + up to 30% of consideration contingent on performance (usually 24 months). Sellers who accept this tend to get 0.5–1x EBITDA higher base price. Seller debt is less common but used strategically. Some high-growth businesses are financed partly by seller notes at 4–5% interest to smooth out cash flow for the buyer. Owner transition clauses: PE buyers increasingly require 12–24 month transition periods where the seller remains involved. This is insurance against key-person risk and helps with customer retention. Expect to negotiate this carefully — longer transitions mean more deal risk for you.

sections.heading: Where are the gaps
sections.body: Lower-mid-market (NZD 1–3M EBITDA) NZ businesses: Underfunded. Not enough PE capital targeting this tier. Local PE firms are focused on larger deals; offshore buyers are cautious about the NZ market. If you're selling a NZD 2M EBITDA business, you'll likely be competing with 3–4 other vendors in a relatively small pool. Trade buyers are usually the best option. Geographically diverse Australian businesses: Overheated competition from PE. If your AU business is in a hotspot sector (healthcare, SaaS), you'll attract multiple bidders. Businesses with single-product dependency: Hard sell. Capital wants platforms with diversification. If 80% of your revenue comes from one customer or product, expect to discount 1–1.5x EBITDA. Businesses with no documented systems: Buyers assume key-person risk and won't pay much. Multiples drop to 3–4x regardless of revenue.

sections.heading: Outlook for H2 2026
sections.body: Expected: Continued selective deal flow. PE capital will be available for high-quality mid-market businesses (NZD 2M+ EBITDA, 20%+ growth, clear buyer universe). Trade buyers will remain the best path for lower-tier and niche businesses. Risk: If interest rates rise or equity markets stumble, deal flow will tighten again. Opportunity: Vendor-financed deals and earn-outs will become more common as buyers hedge their bets. Sellers willing to take deferred consideration may get higher base prices.

title: Healthcare & Professional Services in NZ: Where Growth Happens
excerpt: Consolidation trends, valuation benchmarks, and what acquirers are looking for in allied health, diagnostics, and professional services.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: As we enter May 2026 and mid-year deal activity peaks, healthcare and professional services remain the standout growth sectors in NZ. Demographic tailwinds (aging population, chronic disease prevalence) combined with technology adoption and regulatory changes are creating compounding M&A opportunity. With H1 2026 data now in hand, this article maps where the consolidation is accelerating, what multiples are settling at, and what acquirers are prioritising as they finalise their mid-year acquisition targets.

sections.heading: The consolidation play in healthcare
sections.body: Healthcare networks — multi-clinic operators — are consolidating fast. A typical roll-up model: acquire 3–5 independent clinics in a region, centralise admin and back-office, keep clinical autonomy, realise 20–30% cost savings, and roll up to the next region. Examples: dental networks, physiotherapy/allied health networks, diagnostic networks. Acquirers are paying 6–7.5x EBITDA for these platforms because they have a clear playbook for improvement. Single clinic or single-location practices: valued lower, 4–5.5x, because the acquirer has to build out the roll-up strategy themselves.

sections.heading: Professional services: the niches that move
sections.body: Accounting firms with recurring compliance/tax services: 5–6x EBITDA. Predictable, scalable, lower client concentration risk. Generalist accounting firms: 3–4x. Struggling to differentiate, high founder dependency. Legal practices with a speciality (employment, IP, property): 4.5–5.5x. Generalist practices: harder to place, multiples below 4x. Consulting: only works if you have a proprietary framework and recurring clients (e.g., fractional CFO, operating system coaching). Ad-hoc consulting is a services business (low multiple, high dependency). Recruitment and staffing: niche wins (tech recruitment, executive search): 5–6x. Generalist temp agencies: 3–4.5x with heavy churn risk.

sections.heading: What acquirers prioritise in healthcare/professional services
sections.body: Client retention: This is the #1 metric. If you have 90%+ client renewal, you're golden. Below 85%, buyers get nervous. Recurring revenue model: Clinics with subscription patients, professional services firms with annual retainers, or anything with contractual revenue: these command premiums. Transactional billing: much lower multiple. Systems and processes: Healthcare networks buy platforms so they can replicate and scale. If your clinic is fully dependent on the founder-clinician, it's hard to acquire as part of a roll-up. Owner transition: Acquirers need clarity on whether the founder (particularly if clinically trained) will transition out, stay in clinical role, or move to leadership. This shapes the valuation and integration plan significantly.

sections.heading: NZ-specific dynamics
sections.body: Healthcare is regulated: Familiarity with NZ Health & Disability Commission rules, HCNZ registration, and regional DHB relationships is valuable to offshore acquirers. Professional services: User-friendly Tax Act compliance and accounting standards — these vary from AU and create acquisition friction. Being NZ-based with regulatory expertise is a moat. Location: Auckland clinics/practices command 10–15% premium over regional because of population, client concentration, and acquisition precedent. Regional practices are cheaper but harder to place.

sections.heading: Valuation benchmarks (Q1 2026)
sections.body: Dental/orthodontics: 6–7.5x EBITDA (high recurring, good margins). Allied health (physio, psychology, optometry): 5–6.5x (growing demand, recurring). Diagnostic/imaging networks: 5.5–7x (margins, technology, recurring). Accounting (tax/compliance focus): 5–6x EBITDA. Generalist accounting: 3.5–4.5x. Legal (specialised): 4–5.5x. Legal (generalist): 3–4x. Recruitment (specialist): 4.5–5.5x. Staffing (temp/generalist): 2.5–4x.

sections.heading: Strategic positioning for sale
sections.body: If you're planning an exit in healthcare/professional services: (1) Document your client base and retention. This is your #1 negotiating tool. (2) Build a management layer that's not you. If you're the only clinician or practitioner, the business is valued as a job with goodwill, not a platform. (3) Systemise your service delivery. Can a junior clinician deliver 80% of your services with supervision? That's scalability. (4) De-risk single-client concentration. If 30%+ revenue comes from one client, reduce it before you sell — this alone can cost you 1x EBITDA in the valuation. (5) Understand your buyer universe. Are you attractive to regional/national roll-ups? Are there strategic buyers in your sector? Know who's acquiring and why — it shapes your positioning.

title: Regional Opportunities Beyond Auckland: Infrastructure & Population Plays
excerpt: Population shifts, infrastructure investment, and emerging growth hubs outside Auckland. Where to build and acquire for 2026+.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: August 2026 is the inflection point for several of NZ's major regional infrastructure projects — the Waikato Expressway is weeks from completion, and Bay of Plenty port expansion contracts are being awarded. For a decade, capital in NZ flowed to Auckland. That's shifting faster now. Population growth, infrastructure completion, and housing constraints outside Auckland are creating a closing window of opportunity for acquirers and builders who move before the post-completion valuation reset. This article maps the regional plays and what makes them compelling before the window narrows.

sections.heading: The population shift is real
sections.body: NZ's population growth is increasingly concentrated in secondary cities. Waikato (Hamilton, Cambridge): +2% YoY, led by people priced out of Auckland seeking lower housing costs. Still close enough for AU flights and business travel. Bay of Plenty (Tauranga): +2.5% YoY, driven by retirees and young families. Emerging as a regional hub. Manawatū (Palmerston North): +1.5% YoY, but more volatile. Otago (Dunedin): +0.8% YoY, but growing from a smaller base. This matters because population drives consumption, employment, and business growth. Businesses in these regions are growing at 2x+ the rate of Auckland peers.

sections.heading: Infrastructure tailwinds
sections.body: Waikato Expressway: Major national investment, completion mid-2026. Reduces Auckland-Hamilton commute from 2.5 hrs to <2 hrs. Transforms Hamilton as an Auckland commuter town and distribution hub. Expected impact: 15%+ boost to logistics and hospitality. Bay of Plenty rail: Upgrade and port expansion planned. Makes Tauranga more competitive for export-import logistics. Dunedin: University-led biotech and innovation hub getting government funding. Creates professional services and tech opportunity. These infrastructure events create investment windows — often 12–18 months before completion when property and business multiples are still reasonable, then 12–18 months after when valuations have already priced in the benefit.

sections.heading: Sector opportunities by region
sections.body: Waikato: Distribution and logistics (growth + Expressway tailwind). Hospitality (visitor economy growth). Light manufacturing (lower rents than Auckland, still close to AU port). Healthcare services (aging population + growth). Healthcare is the most reliable regional play across all secondary cities because aging is structural, not cyclical. Bay of Plenty: Hospitality and tourism (Tauranga as a holiday destination). Logistics (port expansion). Retiree-focused services (financial advice, healthcare). Otago: Education and professional services (university). Biotech and life sciences (government-backed hubs). Manawatū: Diversified plays (smaller population base, fewer tailwinds). Agriculture-adjacent businesses (downstream processing, supply).

sections.heading: Valuation arbitrage
sections.body: Regional businesses trade at 15–30% discount to Auckland peers, even with equivalent profitability and growth. Why? Perceived smaller market, higher integration risk for AU buyers, lower buyer density. But if you acquire a $1M EBITDA business in Hamilton at 4.5x (NZD 4.5M), improve it with systems and management depth, and sell it post-Expressway completion, you might exit at 5.5–6x (NZD 5.5–6M). Add population growth, and you've captured both multiple expansion and earnings growth. This is the regional opportunity: buy at a discount, invest in systemisation and growth, and sell into a strengthening market.

sections.heading: Buyer landscape
sections.body: Regional PE: Smaller funds focused on secondary city roll-ups (not enough deal flow for national PE). Often NZ-based or AU-based with regional focus. Less competitive bid processes than Auckland. Trade buyers: Often local operators looking to roll up in their region or consolidate with peers. Can move faster than institutional buyers. AU strategic buyers: Increasingly looking at regional expansion in NZ. Healthcare networks are the exception — they're attracting capital because demographic tailwind is national.

sections.heading: How to play it
sections.body: As an acquirer: (1) Target secondary cities with infrastructure tailwinds 12–18 months before completion. Multiples are still reasonable. (2) Focus on sectors with structural growth (healthcare, logistics, professional services). (3) Plan a 3–5 year hold to capture multiple expansion post-infrastructure. As a seller: (1) Position your regional business as a platform play — not a standalone, but a core asset in a regional roll-up. (2) Emphasise population growth and infrastructure timing. (3) Make your local expertise and customer relationships explicit. (4) Expect to accept slightly lower multiples than Auckland, but plan a faster exit because buyer density is lower (less competition for your deal).

title: Scale Without Breaking: Building Systems That Survive Operator Transitions
excerpt: The difference between founder-dependent businesses and scalable platforms. How to build systems, processes, and layers of management that survive transitions.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: October 2026 brings the final quarter into view — and for business owners planning a 2027 exit, the 12-month clock has started. Most NZ SME owners are trapped in their business: the owner is the business — they make decisions, close deals, solve problems, and hold relationships. This works until it doesn't: the owner wants to exit, brings in a buyer, or simply needs time away. Systemisation is the antidote, but many owners confuse 'having a process' with 'a process that works without the owner.' With Q4 now underway, this article explains the difference and how to build a system that survives operator transitions — in time to matter for a 2027 exit.

sections.heading: The founder-dependent trap
sections.body: Most SME owners don't realise they're trapped until something forces the issue: a health scare, a difficult year, or an acquisition offer that falls apart because the buyer can't see the business working without the founder. Founder dependency looks like: (1) All customer relationships are with the owner. Clients call the owner for decisions, complaints, or reassurance. (2) Institutional knowledge lives in the founder's head. How deals are done, who the trusted suppliers are, where to cut corners in a crunch — it's all implicit. (3) Quality control is the founder's name. Clients trust the founder's work specifically, not the business's standards. (4) Financial systems are loose. The owner knows the margins by feel, not by reporting. (5) Staff defer to the owner for every non-routine decision. The team doesn't have authority to commit resources or change process. Founders often don't see this as a problem. They're busy; they enjoy the control. But the business is undervalued, at risk, and not scalable. The moment the owner is unavailable (sick leave, holiday, acquisition transition), the business falters.

sections.heading: What systemisation actually means
sections.body: Systemisation is not having a manual. (Many businesses have manuals no one reads.) Real systemisation is: (1) Decision rights are distributed. Who can approve a discount? Who can hire? Who can commit to a new supplier? Each decision is mapped to a role, not a person. That role is documented so it survives staff turnover. (2) Customer relationships are structural, not personal. Yes, clients have a primary contact. But contracts, communications, and problem-solving follow documented processes. A client issue doesn't require the founder. (3) Quality is defined and measured. What does 'good' look like for your service or product? How do you know if the team delivered it? This is codified, not implicit in the founder's judgement. (4) Financial data is reliable and timely. You can pull a margin report by customer, by project, by product line. You make decisions from data, not feel. (5) Escalation paths exist and are followed. Problems go up a chain; they don't immediately go to the founder. The team is empowered to solve routine issues. Systemisation means the business runs without daily founder input.

sections.heading: The three layers of systemisation
sections.body: Layer 1: Documentation. Processes exist on paper (or in software). Anyone can read them. This takes 2–4 weeks for a small business (10–20 staff). Most businesses stop here. They document, congratulate themselves, and nothing changes. Layer 2: Compliance and feedback. Teams follow the documented process. You measure whether they're following it (audit, spot-check, review). You give feedback. This is the hard part and takes 2–3 months. You'll discover your process was incomplete or unrealistic. You iterate. Layer 3: Autonomy within boundaries. Once teams consistently follow process and outcomes are good, you loosen oversight. They execute the system with minimal supervision. You step out of the day-to-day. This is where founder value transitions from doing work to improving systems. If a founder stops at Layer 1 or 2, they've created compliance burden, not scalability. The real payoff is Layer 3.

sections.heading: Building a management layer
sections.body: Systemisation only works if someone below the founder has authority to enforce it. This usually means hiring a COO, operations director, or general manager. Characteristics you need: (1) They don't want to be the owner. They're happy in a management role. (2) They're comfortable with ambiguity. You'll be iterating on processes, and they need to build them without waiting for perfection. (3) They have some domain knowledge. They don't need to be an expert in your field, but they need to understand it well enough to spot when processes are broken. (4) They're comfortable giving feedback and making decisions that affect people. This is not a technical role; it's a people role. Hiring a manager is expensive ($80–120k in NZ for a mid-tier COO). It's often the decision point for founders. But it's also the decision point for buyers. A business with a capable COO in place is worth 1–2x EBITDA more because acquisition risk drops. The hire often pays for itself in the improved valuation.

sections.heading: The timeline: how long does this actually take
sections.body: If you commit seriously: 12–18 months to build a functional, Layer 2 system with a manager in place. (3 months to document + 3 months to hire + 6–12 months to embed and iterate). This is not an evening project; it's a major initiative. It requires you to step back from some day-to-day work to do the documentation and provide feedback. You can't build a system while still operating at 100% velocity. Realistically, you'll sacrifice 15–20% of your attention for 6–12 months. The payoff: (1) More freedom (you're not the bottleneck). (2) Higher business valuation (multiples improve 1–2x). (3) Lower stress (you have a team, not just employees). (4) Faster growth (your team can execute without asking you).

sections.heading: Selling a systemic business vs. a founder-dependent one
sections.body: Founder-dependent business, $2M revenue, $300k EBITDA: Might sell at 3–4x (NZD 900k–1.2M). Buyer is nervous about key-person risk. Takes longer to sell; fewer buyers. Systemic business, $2M revenue, $300k EBITDA, with a capable COO in place: Might sell at 5.5–6.5x (NZD 1.65M–1.95M). Buyer sees a platform. The difference is NZD 450k–750k in additional value. For a founder who took 12–18 months to build the system and hired a COO (cost: ~NZD 150k), the ROI is outstanding. Most founders don't realise this maths until it's time to sell. By then, it's too late.

sections.heading: Starting: the first 90 days
sections.body: If you're committing to systemisation: Month 1: Audit your business. Document 5–10 critical processes (order to cash, hiring, customer onboarding, key supplier relationships). This is your starting point. Month 2: Draft standards. What does 'good' look like for each process? Month 3: Pilot with your team. Run two cycles of the process, measuring compliance. Iterate. Then: Hire or promote a manager to own compliance and improvement. This is how it starts. It's not glamorous, but it's foundational.

title: Private Equity in Australasia: What Acquirers Actually Look For
excerpt: PE acquisition thesis, diligence focus, deal structure expectations, and integration planning. What serious buyers care about.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: By March 2026, the first PE deal cycles of the year are underway — and the firms with committed capital are moving. Private equity doesn't acquire the same way strategic buyers do: PE has a thesis, a playbook, and a hard timeline tied to fund cycle. With Q1 deal flow now visible and competition for quality assets already emerging, understanding how PE thinks is critical whether you're selling to PE or trying to attract PE interest before the mid-year window closes.

sections.heading: The PE thesis: value creation timelines
sections.body: PE firms buy to improve and sell. Typical timeline: 3–5 years. They're not buying forever; they're buying to own, improve, and return capital to their fund investors. The thesis determines what they buy. Typical PE playbooks: (1) Operational improvement. Buy a distressed or under-optimised business. Improve margins, reduce costs, systemise. Exit at higher multiple. Example: a hospitality business with 5% margins that PE improves to 10%, then sells at same multiple but higher absolute profit. (2) Bolt-on roll-up. Buy a platform, then acquire 3–5 smaller businesses in the same sector. Realise synergies. Exit as a larger, more valuable platform. Example: one dental clinic becomes a 5-clinic network over 3 years. (3) Growth and scale. Buy a growing business, invest in sales and marketing, expand product lines, enter new geographies. Exit on higher revenue and growth trajectory. Example: a NZD 5M revenue SaaS business becomes NZD 15M+ in 3 years. (4) Financial engineering. Buy a stable, cash-generative business. Use the cash to pay down debt. Exit at the same multiple but with better leverage profile. Less common in current market, but happens. Each thesis shapes what PE looks for.

sections.heading: What PE actually cares about in diligence
sections.body: Customer concentration and retention: Is 30%+ revenue from one customer? If yes, PE is nervous. Can they improve retention? How? Customer acquisition cost: Are you acquiring customers efficiently? Do your payback timelines make sense? If CAC is 18 months and customer lifetime is 3 years, that's risky. Management team: Who runs this business? Are they PE-ready (i.e., can they execute a scaling or improvement thesis)? Or do they need replacement? PE budgets 20–30% of their value creation timeline to management changes, and it's expensive. Margins and cost structure: Where are the costs? Can they be reduced? Is the cost structure scalable, or does it add linearly with revenue? Growth trajectory: Is the business in a growth market? If revenue is flat, PE needs a clear thesis for how they'll drive growth. They don't buy flat businesses unless there's a strong operational fix. Unit economics: For SaaS or subscription: CAC, LTV, churn rate, NRR. For e-commerce: COGS, shipping costs, return rate, repeat purchase rate. For services: project profitability, staff utilisation. PE digs into the unit economics and models different scenarios. Systems and processes: Can the business scale without the owner? Are processes documented? Is there a management layer? This shapes how much PE budgets for operational investment. Cash flow: How much cash does the business actually generate (not EBITDA; actual cash). Are there working capital surprises (seasonality, customer payment terms)? PE models out 5-year cash to understand what they can return to the fund.

sections.heading: The red flags PE will walk away from
sections.body: Single-customer concentration (>50% revenue): Too much risk. Unless the customer is government or highly stable, PE walks. Revenue decline for 2+ consecutive years: PE buys growth or stability. Declining businesses are a value-destruction bet, not a value-creation one. Founder dependency with no transition plan: If the founder is the business and won't stay post-acquisition, PE is nervous. They budget time and cost for transition, but if it's unclear, they move on. Undocumented processes and unknown costs: If due diligence is opaque, PE assumes worst-case. They'll discount 20–30% as 'operational risk.' Regulatory or compliance issues: If there's litigation, environmental liability, or an audit issue, PE will either walk or discount heavily. Customer acquisition is unpredictable or expensive: If you can't explain how you acquire customers or your CAC is >40% of LTV, PE is skeptical of growth thesis. Products/services that are commoditised: If you compete purely on price with 10+ competitors, PE sees low differentiation and margin pressure. They'll lower their acquisition multiple.

sections.heading: Deal structure: what PE expects
sections.body: Purchase price: Typically 70–80% of deal value at close. 20–30% is deferred as earn-out (usually 24 months) or seller note. This is insurance against misrepresentation. Reps and warranties insurance: Seller indemnifies PE for 12–18 months against misrepresentation of facts. This is standard and relatively cheap (0.5–1% of purchase price). Management retention: PE expects the existing management team to stay for 12–24 months post-close to ensure continuity. Founder transition: If the founder is staying, PE expects a documented transition plan (founder steps out of day-to-day over 6–12 months, for example). If the founder is leaving, PE budgets for management replacement and integration. Earnout mechanics: Usually tied to EBITDA targets or growth targets. If the business underperforms, seller receives less deferred consideration. This aligns seller and PE interests but adds risk to seller. Debt: PE usually brings debt financing (40–50% of purchase price from lenders). This is ABL (asset-based lending) or term loan. Seller doesn't carry this, but debt servicing comes from business cash flow, which affects distributions to PE.

sections.heading: Integration playbook
sections.body: Day 1–30: 'Honeymoon' phase. PE, founders, and management align on priorities. No major changes. Days 30–90: Deep-dive on cost structure and processes. PE starts identifying quick wins. Months 3–6: Implement changes (cost reduction, process improvement). Months 6–12: Scale improvements, assess management team. Who's staying? Who needs replacement? Months 12–24: Growth phase. Once cost is stable, PE invests in sales and marketing or new product development. Months 24–36: Prepare for exit. PE prepares for IPO, trade sale, or secondary buyout. This timeline is rough; PE adjusts based on the specific thesis. But the pattern is: stabilise, improve, scale, exit.

sections.heading: Selling to PE: how to position yourself
sections.body: Tell a clear story about value creation. Why would PE be interested? What's the thesis? Is it operational improvement? Roll-up play? Growth and scale? Make it explicit. Highlight the market opportunity and your position. PE cares about tailwind (is the sector growing?). Strong unit economics. If you have them, lead with them. Historical financials and forward guidance. PE models out 5 years; give them clean data to model. Customer concentration and retention data. This is critical. Be transparent about concentration and provide retention data. Management depth. If you have a capable COO or GM, emphasise it. PE is buying a team, not just a business. Growth levers. What's not yet exploited? Where can PE invest to drive growth? A clear answer builds confidence.

sections.heading: PE timing and process
sections.body: Exclusive Process: You work with a PE firm (or a few firms) in a structured exclusive period. Usually 4–8 weeks. Parallel or Competitive Process: You shop the business to multiple PE firms simultaneously. Takes longer (8–12 weeks) but generates competitive tension and higher valuations. Blind Auction: Investment bank runs the process. Many bidders, no exclusivity, shortest timeline (6–8 weeks) but highest competition. For most NZ businesses, blind auction is overblown — there aren't enough local PE buyers. Exclusive or parallel process is more realistic. Expect 8–16 weeks from first contact to term sheet. Then 4–8 weeks of negotiation. Due diligence happens in parallel, adds 6–8 weeks. Total deal cycle: 18–24 weeks from initial approach to close, assuming no surprises.

sections.heading: What NZ businesses get wrong when pitching PE
sections.body: They focus on what the business is, not what PE can build. 'We're a $3M revenue consulting firm' means nothing. 'We're a $3M revenue consulting firm with a proprietary framework, 40% growth, 5-clinic opportunities in the next 2 years' is a PE story. They underestimate customer concentration risk. 'Our top customer is 25% of revenue' should be accompanied by a plan to reduce that risk. Without it, PE discounts 1x EBITDA. They overestimate margins. PE will verify EBITDA independently. Don't try to hide costs; PE will find them. They focus on past and present, not future. PE cares about what they can build. Talk about market opportunity and growth strategy. They don't understand PE motivations. Founders sometimes think PE is buying them. No — PE is buying a business with upside. Align with that.

title: Tax-Efficient Structuring for Business Owners: NZ vs AU Considerations
excerpt: Holding structures, exit tax planning, AU complications, and CGT strategy for NZ owners with AU exposure.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: April is when tax year planning becomes urgent — NZ's financial year has just closed and the decisions you make now will shape your position for the next 12 months. Tax efficiency is not tax avoidance. It's the difference between structuring a business one way and another, with identical economics but different tax outcomes. For NZ owners — particularly those with AU operations or investments — the structuring decisions matter enormously at exit. This article covers the main options and trade-offs before the window to restructure cleanly closes.

sections.heading: NZ sole proprietorship or partnership: the default (often wrong)
sections.body: Many SME owners operate as sole traders or partnerships. It's simple, cheap ($50–200 setup), minimal compliance. Tax: Your business income is assessed at your personal marginal rate (up to 45%). At exit, capital gain is typically tax-free (unless land or financial arrangements). The catch: You have unlimited personal liability for business debts. A liability event (customer sues, contract dispute) can affect personal assets. Most advisers recommend moving out of sole proprietorship once revenue >$500k or risk profile increases. Partnerships are similar: simple but unlimited liability, partners are jointly and severally liable for actions of other partners.

sections.heading: Limited company: the standard play
sections.body: Most NZ SMEs operate as limited companies. Setup: $200–500, annual compliance: $1–3k (depending on complexity). Tax structure: (1) Company earns profit, pays corporate tax (28%). (2) Owner takes dividend, pays personal tax on dividend (up to 45%, reduced by FDR (foreign dividend relief) of 5/19 for most dividends). Net tax: depends on dividend level, but typically 42–45% on profit taken as dividend. At exit: Sale of company shares is tax-free (no capital gains tax in NZ on shares, with limited exceptions). This is huge. If you build a business to $10M value and sell it, the $8M gain is tax-free. But during ownership, income tax is high. Liability: Limited to share capital. Creditors can't pursue personal assets. This is the primary reason to use a company.

sections.heading: Multi-layer structures: holding company + operating company
sections.body: Many larger businesses use a two-layer structure: (1) Operating Company: Runs the business, earns profit, pays tax (28%). (2) Holding Company: Owns the operating company, receives dividends from operating company (tax-free if intercompany), distributes to owner. Why? (1) Asset protection. The holding company owns the operating company as an asset. If something goes wrong operationally, liability is isolated. (2) Exit flexibility. You can sell just the operating company (full sale) or the holding company (full exit). Different tax and transaction costs. (3) Dividend streaming. Allows more flexibility in how profits flow and are taxed at different levels. At exit: If you sell the operating company, the holding company retains the cash and any other assets. If you sell the holding company shares, you exit cleanly. Tax: More complex than single company, but if structured well, more flexible. Setup cost: $500–1,500. Annual compliance: $2–5k (double compliance).

sections.heading: Australian operations: the complication
sections.body: If you have an AU subsidiary or AU operations: AU tax residency kicks in. Your AU entity is taxed in AU on AU-source income (45% corporate tax, 47% top personal rate). If your NZ holding company receives dividends from the AU entity, those dividends are taxed in NZ (usually with some foreign investment fund tax relief, but it's complicated). Cross-border transactions (inter-company charges, royalties, management fees) are scrutinised by tax authorities. Transfer pricing rules (pricing transactions between NZ and AU entities) require documentation. If not documented properly, authorities can adjust prices, creating double taxation. The takeaway: Operating in both NZ and AU requires professional tax planning. This is where generic advice breaks down. You need a tax adviser familiar with both jurisdictions.

sections.heading: Exit tax planning: the critical phase
sections.body: Most owners don't think about exit tax until they're selling. By then, options are limited. Better approach: Plan the exit structure 12–24 months before you sell. Key decisions: (1) What will you sell? (Full company, assets, or a portion?). (2) Will any proceeds stay in a holding company (reinvestment, cash buffer) or come out as personal cash? (3) Are you staying involved post-exit (earn-out, consulting)? Each decision has tax implications. Example: If you sell your operating company for $5M, and the buyer wants the real estate too, you might separate the real estate into a separate entity and lease it to the buyer (passive income, capital preservation). Or you might sell everything and reinvest in a holding company (fresh start, clean exit). These aren't routine decisions; they need planning.

sections.heading: Capital gains planning for NZ business owners
sections.body: NZ doesn't have capital gains tax on shares (with exceptions for dealers, financial arrangements, land, or short-term speculation). But if you're in the business of buying and selling businesses, the Commissioner may argue your gains are income, not capital. There's also the bright-line test for real estate (1–5 year holds). Most SME operators are safe: You're building one business long-term. Selling it is a capital event, not a profit-making scheme. But if you're a serial acquirer or property speculator, tax authority scrutiny increases. The lesson: Be clear about intent. If you're building a business to exit once, not multiple times, document that intent. It supports the capital gains tax-free position.

sections.heading: Retirement planning: KiwiSaver and structuring
sections.body: Many SME owners neglect KiwiSaver because they're in a company structure and KiwiSaver employer contribution is limited ($3,720/year cap). But KiwiSaver is tax-efficient for both employee and employer contributions. Strategies: (1) Max out employer contributions (cheaper than paying the owner as salary + KiwiSaver by the owner). (2) Use KiwiSaver as a long-term exit strategy: accumulate to retirement, live off KiwiSaver + other investments. (3) If exiting at age 55–60, consider timing to minimise personal income tax during the exit (e.g., exit year might be high income; structuring proceeds to defer some to following years can reduce tax). This requires coordination with accountant and financial planner.

sections.heading: NZ vs AU tax efficiency: the comparison
sections.body: NZ: Capital gains on shares are tax-free (huge advantage). Corporate tax 28% (competitive). Personal rates up to 45% (high). AU: Capital gains tax on shares is 50% discount if held >1 year (better than NZ rate if you're in 47% bracket, worse if you're in lower bracket). Corporate tax 30% (slightly higher than NZ). Personal rates up to 47% (slightly higher). Dividend tax: Both NZ and AU have franking/imputation systems (you get credit for company tax paid). Overall: NZ has slight tax advantage for business exit (zero CGT on shares). AU has slight advantage during ownership if you take dividends (franking provides credit). For a NZ owner with AU operations, the structuring trade-off is usually: build in NZ (tax-efficient during ownership), sell NZ company (no CGT), and reinvest in AU if desired.

sections.heading: Action steps: tax-efficient structuring
sections.body: If you're currently a sole trader or partnership: Migrate to a company structure. Cost ~$200–500 now; benefit is unlimited liability protection and better exit optionality. If you're in a company and planning exit >12 months out: Engage a tax adviser to plan the exit structure. Decisions now (e.g., separate real estate, create holding company) are cheaper than changes during the sale. If you have AU operations: Get professional tax advice on holding structure. Transfer pricing documentation must be in place. If you're planning AIP or investor visa: Structure your investments in a holding company that satisfies INZ active involvement requirements. Single-purpose entities for each investment may be necessary. If you're a serial operator (multiple businesses over time): Document your intent for each. Each business should be clearly separate with clear entry/exit documentation. This supports the 'capital gain' (not 'income') tax position.
